drills.scenario: Maria runs a Shopify pet-treats brand. She wants to generate actual purchases on her product page and has the Facebook pixel installed and firing on the 'Purchase' event. A friend tells her to just 'run a Traffic campaign because more clicks means more sales eventually.'
drills.question: Which objective should Maria choose, and why is the Traffic advice flawed?
options: Traffic — cheap clicks always lead to sales once volume is high enough
Conversions, optimized for the Purchase event — Traffic optimizes for cheap clicks, not buyers
Engagement — purchases follow from post engagement
Brand Awareness — buyers need to recognize the brand first
drills.explanation: The Conversions objective (with the pixel installed) tells Facebook's AI to show ads to people highly likely to purchase, and reports cost per conversion. Traffic optimizes for the cheapest clicks, not buyers — the 'enough traffic will convert something' mindset is a documented pitfall: you get tons of cheap clicks and no conversions because the ad did exactly what you asked.

drills.scenario: A boutique coffee roaster is hosting a TICKETED tasting workshop — attendees must pay $25 to register. The owner is about to set up an 'Event Responses' campaign because Facebook will auto-add it to people's calendars.
drills.question: What's the correct objective choice here?
options: Event Responses — calendar adds drive paid attendance just as well
Reach — maximize how many people see the event
Conversions — the rule is to use Conversions, not Event Responses, for paid events
Brand Awareness — build interest in the workshop first
drills.explanation: Event Responses targets people likely to join a free Facebook-hosted event and auto-adds it to their calendar — great for free public events, grand openings, festivals. But the module's explicit rule is: for PAID events, use Conversions instead, so optimization aligns with actual ticket purchases.

drills.scenario: A brand-new B2B coach has driven 0 conversions so far and wants to follow the Facebook Flight Plan from the very beginning. He's overwhelmed and asks which two simple campaigns to run first to gain momentum and — most importantly — DATA.
drills.question: For Stage 1 (0 to 100 conversions), which two campaigns does the Flight Plan prescribe?
options: A Dynamic Product Ad and a retargeting hot-audience video
A 'Like' Ad campaign and a Website Conversions Link Post Ad
A Long Copy Link Post Ad and a CTA Video Ad
A Store Visits campaign and a Lead Ad to warm audiences
drills.explanation: Flight Plan Stage 1 (takeoff, 0–100 conversions) runs only two proven, novice-doable campaigns: a 'Like' Ad campaign (page-like, no external destination) and a Website Conversions Link Post Ad driving to a simple landing page. The goal is momentum, traffic, conversions, and above all DATA. Video, Long Copy, DPAs and retargeting come later in Stages 2 and 3.

drills.scenario: An online fitness coach is about to build her first Page Likes campaign inside Ads Manager and wants to follow Facebook's standard creation sequence correctly.
drills.question: Put the build steps in the correct order, from first to last.
options: At the CAMPAIGN level, set the objective (Page Likes) and name it descriptively
At the AD SET level, define the audience, placements, and budget
At the AD level, choose the images and write the copy
Place the order — then remember the campaign is created PAUSED, so activate it
drills.explanation: Facebook's three-level build sequence is Campaign > Ad Set > Ad: first set the objective at the Campaign level, then define audience/placements/budget at the Ad Set level, then build the creative at the Ad level. After ordering, new campaigns are always created paused, so the final step is to activate it (optionally adding more ad sets first).

drills.scenario: A skincare brand boosted a short video that went mildly viral — it shows roughly 1,250 reactions and 1,280 shares (about a 1:1 Like-to-Share ratio). The team's sole goal is sales, so they instinctively want to run it under the Conversions objective. In a prior test, the same video on Video Views delivered a $17.68 CPA vs. $19.45 CPA on Conversions, despite Conversions having a higher on-page conversion rate.
drills.question: What's the most defensible decision given the module's guidance?
options: Always use Conversions for any sales video — CPA differences are noise
Test Video Views (or Engagement) against Conversions — a viral ~1:1 video can win on cheaper, broader reach and lower CPA
Switch to Brand Awareness since the video is viral
Use Reach to cap frequency and protect the viral momentum
drills.explanation: A super-engaging/viral video with a ~1:1 (or better) Like-to-Share ratio can deliver better ROI/CPA on Video Views or Page Post Engagement because Facebook serves a larger, broader audience at much lower CPM/CPC — exactly what the S3 Sauce case showed ($17.68 vs $19.45 CPA even with a lower sales-page CVR). There's no hard rule, so the correct move is to TEST rather than reflexively default to Conversions.

drills.scenario: A local family-owned restaurant with a single location wants to drive nearby people to walk in this month on a small budget. The owner asks which tactics from the module genuinely fit a single-location local business.
drills.question: Select ALL choices that are appropriate for this scenario.
options: Use the Reach objective with location targeting and 'Everyone in this location' to reach people traveling nearby, capping frequency
Run a small-budget branding boosted post (e.g., 'mention Facebook, get a free appetizer') for top-of-mind awareness
Set up the Store Visits objective and bulk-upload all locations to dynamically update ads per location
Allocate 5–10% of budget to a Page Like campaign to build a warm fan base with higher newsfeed frequency
drills.explanation: Reach with location targeting (and 'Everyone in this location') suits local/physical businesses; a small branding boosted post builds top-of-mind awareness; and a 5–10% Page Like allocation builds a warm, higher-frequency fan base. Store Visits is designed for MULTI-location businesses (it bulk-adds many locations and updates ads per location) — overkill and mismatched for a single-location restaurant.

drills.scenario: A SaaS founder launched a campaign two weeks ago to drive trial signups. Reports show huge social shares and lots of post engagement, but almost no trials. Reviewing the setup, you notice the campaign objective is set to Page Post Engagement.
drills.question: What's the first thing to diagnose, and what's the fix?
options: The creative is bad — rewrite the ad copy and keep the same objective
The audience is wrong — narrow targeting but keep Page Post Engagement
The objective doesn't match the goal — you can't change it live, so rebuild under a Conversion objective optimized for signups
The budget is too low — increase spend on the existing campaign
drills.explanation: Wrong objective = wrong result: running Page Post Engagement when you want signups gets you engagement, not conversions. The troubleshooting rule is to first check that the objective matches the goal when you see high shares but low conversions. Since you cannot change the objective on a live campaign, you must rebuild from scratch under a Conversion objective optimized for the signup event.
